content:
You are a creative and professional JavaScript game developer who builds polished and visually appealing video games using p5.js primitives.
Your game should offer a fun and engaging game experience for the player following game development principles.
Create a learnable progression ramp that balances learning, fun, and challenge.
Use your creativity to enrich the concept into an innovative design before coding, adding interesting and consistent elements beyond the game concept.
Ensure the implementation is free of inconsistencies and errors. Write modular ES6 code with automated tests covering key aspects of the game.
Follow the hard constraints exactly; violating any is a critical error.

<hard_constraints>
- The full game can be expanded by adding more elements to the game concept but should align with the game concept respecting the hard constraints and game design principles.
- Game must load and function without errors, start on pressing ENTER, gameplay must be responsive to player inputs, and have correct game phase handling.
- Game phases: START→PLAYING (ENTER), PLAYING→GAME_OVER (win/lose), GAME_OVER→START (R), PLAYING↔PAUSED (ESC). Expose this in a variable called `gameState.gamePhase` where `gameState` is a global object accessible via `window.getGameState()`.
- Start screen should have a title, short description of the game, instructions to play the game, and a "PRESS ENTER TO START" prompt.
- Allowed libraries: p5.js, p5.collide2D
- Canvas: `600 × 400 px`; target 60 FPS
- Keyboard inputs only. No mouse control.
- Allowed gameplay control keys: Arrow keys (37-40), Space (32), Shift (16), Z (90)
- Game phase specific controls:
    - ENTER (13)  – start the game
    - ESC (27)    – pause / unpause
    - R (82)      – restart; returns to the start screen, where the player can press ENTER to play again
- No external images, sprites, fonts, or other assets. Make visual assets and animations using code.
- No audio or sound effects.
- Use p5.js in instance mode and expose the game instance globally as window.gameInstance.
- Ensure game reproducibility using `p.randomSeed(42)` in the `setup` function. No other random seeding.
- Use ES6 syntax with proper imports and exports at the top of the file where they are used. No dynamic imports or require() imports.
- Maintain `p.logs` as write-only; log game_info, inputs, player_info with proper initialization and updates during the game loop.
</hard_constraints>

<instructions>
<game_concept_to_game_design>
Expand the game concept with new game elements with a well-planned progression and learning curve that enables the player to learn about the game elements and mechanics.
For inspiration, following are some examples of game elements that you can add to the game design as long as the final game satisfies the requirements of the game concept:
- environment: game setting, genre, viewpoint
- entities: characters, opponents, collaborators, NPCs, objects in the game, power-ups, destructible objects, obstacles
- mechanics: rules of the games, final win conditions, game over conditions, subgoals, rewards, player actions and abilities, interaction between entities
- graphics: visual appearance and animations of the game entities and the environment
</game_concept_to_game_design>

<code_instructions>
You are encouraged to write as much code as you can to make a fun game, aesthetically appealing, and provide a fun gameplay experience for the player when playing the game for the first time.

<code_organization>
- Modular code organization with proper ES6 imports:
  - Organize the code into multiple files as needed (game.js, globals.js, etc.)
  - Group related functionality together in meaningful modules
  - Proper imports:
    - Use ES6 for imports and exports
    - Every file MUST import ALL constants, variables, functions, classes, and external symbols at the top of every file. No Node.js style require imports. No dynamic imports within functions
```javascript
// Essential pattern (ALWAYS follow this)
import { Player, Enemy } from './entities.js';
import { checkCollision, applyPhysics } from './physics.js';
import { gameState, CANVAS_WIDTH } from './globals.js';

// Example function with proper dependencies
function createPlayer(x, y) {
  const player = new Player(x, y);
  gameState.player = player;
  gameState.entities.push(player);
  return player;
}
```

- State Management:
  - Implement `function getGameState()` and attach it to `window`. It must return the `gameState` object.
  - Use a single `gameState` object to track the game's current data and status. Store all game data in the `gameState` object to be used by automated testing code to play-test the game.    
```javascript
export const gameState = {
  player: null,       // player entity to be initialized in the setup function and initialized before the game starts
  entities: [],       // all game entities including player
  score: 0,
  gamePhase: "START", // "START", "PLAYING", "GAME_OVER_WIN/LOSE", "PAUSED"
  controlMode: "HUMAN", // "HUMAN", "TEST_1", "TEST_2", "TEST_3", etc.
  ... // other game state variables which are needed to be tracked for automated testing
};
```
</code_organization>

<p5_instructions>
- Use p5.js in instance mode and store the p5 instance in a variable called `gameInstance`. Expose the game instance globally as follows:
```javascript
const p5 = window.p5
let gameInstance = new p5(p => {
    // Initialize variables
    ...
    // Initialize the logs. Important: Do not reset the logs at any point in the code! logs are considered write-only!
    p.logs = {
        "game_info": [],  // Information about the game
        "inputs": [],     // Information about the key inputs
        "player_info": [] // Information about the player character
    };

    // Functions
    ...
});
// Expose the game instance globally
window.gameInstance = gameInstance;
```
- p.logs is write-only. Do not reset it. We want to keep track of all the logs since the start of the p5.js game instance.
- Variables to store in the p.logs during gameplay:
  - In "game_info": Information about the game when there are changes in the game phases
    - "data": Any data like game phase or anything else you want to log
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event accessed using `p.frameCount` and `Date.now()` respectively
  - In "inputs": Store the control inputs when they are triggered by the player
    - "input_type": The input event type (keyPressed, keyReleased, etc.)
    - "data": Additional data specific to the input type. Store `key` and `keyCode` if the input is a key.
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event
  - In "player_info": Information about the player character when there are changes in the player's state (accessed via `gameState.player` where `gameState` is accessible via `window.getGameState()`)
    - "screen_x", "screen_y": The x and y position of the player on the screen
    - "game_x", "game_y": The x and y position of the player in the game world
    - "framecount": The framecount of the event

- Useful Functions:
  - Use p. to call p5 functions.
  - p5: `keyIsDown(key_code)`, `noLoop()/loop()`, `tint()`, `push()/pop()`, `textAlign()`, `textSize()`, `text()`
  - p5.collide2d for collision detection
    - Note that the specific order of the shapes in the function name matters. For example, 'collideCircleRect' is not available.
    - Available functions: `collidePointPoint`, `collidePointCircle`, `collidePointEllipse`, `collidePointRect`, `collidePointLine`, `collidePointArc`, `collideRectRect`, `collideCircleCircle`, `collideRectCircle`, `collideLineLine`, `collideLineCircle`, `collideLineRect`, `collidePointPoly`, `collideCirclePoly`, `collideRectPoly`, `collideLinePoly`, `collidePolyPoly`, `collidePointTriangle`
</p5_instructions>

<game_mechanics_instructions>
Implement intuitive, believable mechanics that enable players to achieve the final goal.
Choose parameters and logic for game mechanics that are intuitive and believable. These can change as the game progresses. 
Even at the end of the game, the mechanics must be such that the player can win by showing their learned skill.
- Controls:
  - Player controls must be intuitive and impact the game state in a meaningful way
  - Input handling: Use keyboard for HUMAN mode, automated testing action outputs for automated testing modes
  - When automated testing mode is selected, the game must automatically play using the action outputs from the automated testing controller
- Non-player characters and objects:
  - NPCs and objects must be spawned and behave respecting the progression of the game
  - Implement intelligent, intuitive, and challenging behavior for all game entities to keep the player engaged
- Interactions with other entities and objects:
  - Have plausible physics with parameters and logic to give it a believable realistic feel
  - Prevent objects from passing through each other or behaving inconsistently to prevent shocking the player
- GAME_OVER Conditions:
  - Implement mechanics that can lead to win or lose conditions achievable by the player
  - GAME_OVER must be a final state followed by game over screen
- Rewards:
  - Define meaningful and reasonable conditions for rewards to keep the player motivated to reach the final goal
  - Do not excessively repeat the same rewards
</game_mechanics_instructions>

<rendering_instructions>
- Implement aesthetic objects, smooth animations, and dynamic effects for the game environment, entities, and their interactions
- Provide useful visual feedback:
  - Player character must be distinctly identifiable
  - Provide visual feedback for interactions and response to their actions
  - UI: Add well-positioned text and graphics to inform the player about information like score, health, etc.
  - Never display current controlMode (HUMAN/TESTING) on the game canvas.
- Ensure the correct render order of the game objects to ensure player is visible on the screen
- Avoid flickering:
  - Do not call p.randomSeed() inside draw()
  - Have exactly one call to p.background() at the top of draw()
  - Do not draw elements that are randomly sampled at every frame as this causes flickering
  - Use ternary operator to set colors for conditional rendering: Example: `p.fill(...(CONDITION ? [255, 220, 150] : [40, 30, 20]));`
- Required Informative Game Screens:
  - Start: Title, clear instructions, objectives, and controls with actions for the player, "PRESS ENTER TO START"
  - Playing: Game rendering, score, and active gameplay
  - Pause: Small text "PAUSED" indicator in top right
  - Game Over: Win/Lose message, final score, "PRESS R TO RESTART", R takes you back to the start screen
</rendering_instructions>

</code_instructions>
</instructions>


<task>
Implement a complete, fun, and error-free p5.js game for the following concept:
{
  "original_game_name": "Gossip Harbor\u00ae: Merge & Story",
  "game_name": "Harbor Merge Mystery",
  "genre": "Merge Puzzle, Narrative Puzzle",
  "core_mechanics": "The game operates on a grid-based merge system. Players interact primarily through simulated 'taps' (mouse clicks) and 'drags' (mouse drags).\n\n**Player Actions & Triggers:**\n*   **Select Item:** Player clicks (mouse press) on an item located in a grid cell. This item is then 'picked up'.\n*   **Move Item:** While holding down the mouse button (mouse dragged), the selected item follows the mouse cursor.\n*   **Drop Item:** Player releases the mouse button (mouse released). The item is dropped into the grid cell under the cursor.\n*   **Fulfill Order:** Player drags a required item directly to a designated 'Order Zone' area on the UI and releases it.\n*   **UI Interaction:** Player clicks on buttons (e.g., 'Play', 'Pause', 'Resume', 'Quit to Menu').\n\n**Rules & Systems:**\n*   **Merge Board:** A 2D array (`grid[row][col]`) represents the game board. Each element stores data for an item (or `null` if empty): `item_id`, `level`, `x_pos`, `y_pos`.\n*   **Item Generation:** New items (always Level 1) are periodically spawned into a random empty grid cell. The spawn rate decreases (becomes faster) with higher levels. New items can also be manually generated after fulfilling orders (to incentivize progression).\n*   **Merging:**\n    1.  When an item is dropped, the system checks if it lands on an identical item (same `item_id` AND same `level`).\n    2.  If two identical items (Level N) are adjacent or one is dropped onto another, they combine. The 'target' item (or the cell where they combined) is replaced by a single item of `level N+1`. The 'source' item's cell becomes empty.\n    3.  Each item type (`item_id`) has a maximum merge level (e.g., Level 6).\n*   **Customer Orders:** A list of active orders is displayed. Each order specifies an `item_id`, `level`, and `quantity` required. \n*   **Order Fulfillment:** When an item matching an active order is dropped into the 'Order Zone':\n    1.  The item is consumed (removed from the board).\n    2.  The `quantity` for that order decreases. If `quantity` reaches 0, the order is marked as complete.\n    3.  Points and currency are awarded. New orders may appear to replace completed ones.\n*   **Currency & Progression:** Fulfilling orders earns 'currency' (which is abstracted into progression points). Accumulating enough progression points automatically triggers the next 'story segment' (level transition).\n*   **Board Full Check:** If the board becomes completely full, and there are no possible merge combinations for any existing items, the game triggers a 'lose' condition.",
  "visual_elements": "All visual elements will be rendered using p5.js drawing primitives and potentially simple pre-loaded sprite assets for items.\n\n**Game Objects:**\n*   **Merge Grid Cells:** Rendered as square `rect()` shapes with a darker background color (e.g., `(50, 50, 50)`) and a lighter border (e.g., `(100, 100, 100)`).\n*   **Merge Items:** Each item is a distinct visual. Initially, basic shapes (`ellipse()` for coffee, `rect()` for sandwiches, `triangle()` for pastries) with unique colors. As items merge to higher levels, their visual complexity increases:\n    *   Level 1: Simple shape, solid color.\n    *   Level 2: Same shape, different color, perhaps a small icon.\n    *   Level 3: More detailed icon/sprite (e.g., a 'latte' instead of just a 'coffee bean').\n    *   Level 4+: Increasingly detailed sprites, perhaps with small visual flourishes (e.g., steam for coffee, grill marks for a sandwich).\n*   **Selected Item:** When an item is selected, it will have a distinct highlight (e.g., a glowing `stroke()` or a larger `fill()` color).\n*   **Customer Order UI:** A rectangular panel, typically on the side of the screen, displaying smaller icons of the items required for current orders, alongside text indicating the `quantity` (`e.g., 'x2'`). Completed orders might fade out or have a checkmark.\n*   **Order Zone:** A clearly marked area on the board (e.g., a pulsating border or dedicated square) where items are dropped to fulfill orders.\n*   **Currency/Score/Level Display:** Text elements (`text()`) displayed in a prominent, fixed position on the UI.\n*   **Story/Dialogue Box:** A semi-transparent rectangular overlay with text (`text()`) for narrative segments and level transitions.\n*   **UI Buttons:** `rect()` shapes with `text()` labels (e.g., 'Play', 'Pause', 'Resume', 'Menu').\n*   **Game Background:** A simple solid color (`background()`) or a subtle texture/gradient.\n\n**Animations & Visual Effects:**\n*   **Item Selection:** A subtle `glow` effect (e.g., `stroke()` color shift, increased `strokeWeight()`) on the selected item.\n*   **Item Movement:** Smooth interpolation of `x, y` coordinates as the item follows the mouse, not just snapping.\n*   **Merge Effect:** A brief visual flash (e.g., a burst of white pixels or a scaling effect) and an optional sound effect at the point of merge.\n*   **Order Fulfilled:** The fulfilled item visually scales down and fades out (reduced `alpha` value) as it's consumed. The corresponding order icon in the UI might briefly pulse or animate.\n*   **Item Generation:** New items fading in (`alpha` interpolation) when they appear on the board.\n*   **Level Transition:** The screen fades to a solid color, then displays level transition text, then fades back to the game board.\n*   **Score/Currency Update:** A small upward 'pop' or brief color change for the displayed numbers when points/currency are awarded.",
  "control_scheme": "The primary control mechanism is the mouse for core gameplay. Keyboard controls are provided for UI navigation and secondary actions.\n\n*   **Mouse Left Click (Press):** Selects an item on the grid or activates a UI button.\n*   **Mouse Drag:** Moves the currently selected item across the grid or towards the 'Order Zone'.\n*   **Mouse Release:** Drops the selected item into a grid cell or the 'Order Zone', triggering merge or order fulfillment checks.\n\n*   **Spacebar:** Toggles Pause/Resume game state during `PLAYING`.\n*   **ArrowUp / ArrowDown:** Navigates menu options in `MENU` or `PAUSED` states (e.g., selecting 'Play' or 'Resume').\n*   **Z:** Confirms a selected menu option or acknowledges a dialogue box (e.g., 'continue' button).",
  "game_states": "The game will transition through several distinct states to manage flow and user experience.\n\n1.  **MENU_STATE:**\n    *   **Description:** The initial screen displaying the game title, a 'Play' button, and potentially a 'High Scores' option.\n    *   **Triggers:** Game launch; player selects 'Quit to Menu' from `PAUSED` state.\n    *   **Transitions:**\n        *   `MENU_STATE` -> `PLAYING_STATE`: Player clicks 'Play' button.\n\n2.  **PLAYING_STATE:**\n    *   **Description:** The main gameplay loop where players interact with the merge board, fulfill orders, and progress through the story.\n    *   **Triggers:** Entering from `MENU_STATE` or `PAUSED_STATE`, or after a `LEVEL_TRANSITION_STATE`.\n    *   **Transitions:**\n        *   `PLAYING_STATE` -> `PAUSED_STATE`: Player presses `Space` or clicks a 'Pause' button.\n        *   `PLAYING_STATE` -> `LEVEL_TRANSITION_STATE`: Player completes all orders/progression goals for the current level.\n        *   `PLAYING_STATE` -> `GAME_OVER_STATE`: Player triggers the lose condition (board full, no merges).\n        *   `PLAYING_STATE` -> `WIN_STATE`: Player completes all levels.\n\n3.  **PAUSED_STATE:**\n    *   **Description:** Game execution is halted. A semi-transparent overlay shows 'Paused' and options like 'Resume', 'Quit to Menu'.\n    *   **Triggers:** Player presses `Space` or clicks a 'Pause' button during `PLAYING_STATE`.\n    *   **Transitions:**\n        *   `PAUSED_STATE` -> `PLAYING_STATE`: Player clicks 'Resume' button.\n        *   `PAUSED_STATE` -> `MENU_STATE`: Player clicks 'Quit to Menu' button.\n\n4.  **LEVEL_TRANSITION_STATE:**\n    *   **Description:** Displays a 'Level Complete!' message, followed by a brief text snippet for the next story segment/renovation task, and then loads the next level's configuration.\n    *   **Triggers:** All current level objectives are met during `PLAYING_STATE`.\n    *   **Transitions:**\n        *   `LEVEL_TRANSITION_STATE` -> `PLAYING_STATE`: Automatically after a short delay (e.g., 2-3 seconds) once the next level data is loaded.\n\n5.  **GAME_OVER_STATE:**\n    *   **Description:** Displays 'Game Over!' message, final score, and options to 'Try Again' (restart current level) or 'Quit to Menu'.\n    *   **Triggers:** Lose condition is met during `PLAYING_STATE` (board full, no merges).\n    *   **Transitions:**\n        *   `GAME_OVER_STATE` -> `PLAYING_STATE`: Player clicks 'Try Again' (restarts the level they lost on).\n        *   `GAME_OVER_STATE` -> `MENU_STATE`: Player clicks 'Quit to Menu'.\n\n6.  **WIN_STATE:**\n    *   **Description:** Displays 'Congratulations! You Restored Gossip Harbor!' message, final score, and option to 'Play Again' (start new game) or 'Quit to Menu'.\n    *   **Triggers:** Player completes the final level's objectives during `PLAYING_STATE`.\n    *   **Transitions:**\n        *   `WIN_STATE` -> `MENU_STATE`: Player clicks 'Play Again' or 'Quit to Menu'.",
  "win_conditions": "The player wins the game by successfully completing all the required orders and objectives across all 5 predefined levels. This signifies the full restoration of the restaurant and the resolution of the island's mystery.",
  "lose_conditions": "The player loses the game if the merge board becomes completely full (no empty grid cells available) AND there are no possible merge combinations for any two existing items on the board. This state prevents new item generation or any further gameplay actions, effectively ending progression.",
  "scoring_system": "The game incorporates a dynamic scoring system to reward player actions and progression. The score will be displayed prominently in the top-right corner of the screen, formatted as `SCORE: XXXXXX`.\n\n*   **Merging Items:**\n    *   `+10 points * (Level of Resulting Item)`: Whenever two items merge, points are awarded based on the level of the *new*, higher-level item created. For example, two Level 1 items merging to a Level 2 item yields `10 * 2 = 20` points. Two Level 4 items merging to a Level 5 item yields `10 * 5 = 50` points.\n*   **Fulfilling Orders:**\n    *   `+50 points + (10 points * Level of Ordered Item)`: When a player successfully delivers an item to fulfill a customer order, a base of 50 points is awarded, plus an additional 10 points for each level of the item requested. For example, fulfilling an order for a Level 3 item yields `50 + (10 * 3) = 80` points.\n*   **Completing a Level:**\n    *   `+500 bonus points`: A significant bonus is awarded upon successful completion of all objectives for a given level.\n\n**Bonus Points/Multipliers:** No additional bonus points or multipliers are implemented for simplicity.\n\n**High Score System:** A high score system will be implemented using `localStorage` to persist the top score across browser sessions. The high score will be displayed on the `MENU_STATE`.\n\n**Score Affects Gameplay:** The score does not directly affect gameplay. It serves as a metric for player achievement and comparison.",
  "level_system": "The game features 5 distinct levels, each representing a stage of the restaurant's restoration and the unfolding mystery. Difficulty increases progressively through each level.\n\n**Level Indicator:** A 'Level X / 5' display will be shown prominently on the UI during `PLAYING_STATE` (e.g., bottom-left).\n\n**Transition Display:** When transitioning between levels (`LEVEL_TRANSITION_STATE`), the screen will display 'LEVEL COMPLETE!' followed by 'Loading Level X+1: [Short Story Snippet]' for 2-3 seconds, then fade into the next level.\n\n--- \n\n**Level 1: The Crumbling Start**\n*   **Difficulty:** Easy\n*   **Board Size:** 5x5 grid.\n*   **Initial Items:** 5-7 low-level items (Level 1-2) randomly placed.\n*   **Order Complexity:** Simple, focusing on 3-5 orders of Level 1-2 items (e.g., '2 Coffees', '1 Sandwich').\n*   **New Item Spawn Rate:** 1 item every 10 seconds.\n*   **Story Snippet:** 'Welcome to Gossip Harbor, Quinn! This old diner needs a lot of work. Let's start with some basic coffee orders to get the word out.'\n*   **Advancement:** Fulfill 5 total orders.\n\n**Level 2: Basic Renovations**\n*   **Difficulty:** Medium\n*   **Board Size:** 5x5 grid.\n*   **Initial Items:** 7-10 low to mid-level items (up to Level 3).\n*   **Order Complexity:** 5-7 orders, introducing Level 3 items and slightly higher quantities (e.g., '3 Croissants', '2 Level 3 Sandwiches').\n*   **New Item Spawn Rate:** 1 item every 8 seconds (slightly faster).\n*   **Story Snippet:** 'Great start! We've made enough to patch up the flooring. Now, customers are asking for more breakfast items. Get those pastries and fancier sandwiches ready!'\n*   **Advancement:** Fulfill 7 total orders.\n\n**Level 3: Kitchen Expansion**\n*   **Difficulty:** Medium-Hard\n*   **Board Size:** 6x6 grid (increased complexity and space for more items).\n*   **Initial Items:** 10-12 mid-level items (up to Level 4).\n*   **Order Complexity:** 7-9 orders, requiring Level 4 items and sometimes multiple different items for a single order (e.g., '1 Level 4 Coffee & 2 Level 2 Pastries').\n*   **New Item Spawn Rate:** 1 item every 7 seconds.\n*   **Story Snippet:** 'The kitchen is a mess! We need better equipment to serve more elaborate dishes. Some locals are talking, subtle hints of a secret... keep merging those high-level ingredients!'\n*   **Advancement:** Fulfill 9 total orders.\n\n**Level 4: Outdoor Seating & Mystery Clues**\n*   **Difficulty:** Hard\n*   **Board Size:** 6x6 grid.\n*   **Initial Items:** 12-15 mixed-level items (up to Level 5), with a higher density of Level 3-4 items.\n*   **Order Complexity:** 9-12 orders, frequently requiring Level 5 items and complex combinations. More concurrent orders active.\n*   **New Item Spawn Rate:** 1 item every 6 seconds.\n*   **Story Snippet:** 'The outdoor seating is a hit! But the regulars are starting to talk... and it's not just about the food. Someone knows something about the island's secret. We need to complete more orders to earn their trust and uncover clues!'\n*   **Advancement:** Fulfill 12 total orders.\n\n**Level 5: Grand Reopening & The Reveal**\n*   **Difficulty:** Very Hard\n*   **Board Size:** 7x7 grid (maximum complexity and item capacity).\n*   **Initial Items:** 15-20 high-level items (up to Level 6), densely populated board.\n*   **Order Complexity:** 12-15 challenging orders, demanding Level 6+ (max level) items, and intricate, multi-item customer requests. High pressure to manage board space.\n*   **New Item Spawn Rate:** 1 item every 5 seconds (fastest).\n*   **Story Snippet:** 'The day of the grand reopening! Everything depends on this. The truth about the island mystery is within reach. Serve every customer and solve the puzzle once and for all!'\n*   **Advancement:** Fulfill 15 total orders.",
  "simplifications": "To create a manageable HTML5 game using p5.js, several features from the original 'Gossip Harbor\u00ae: Merge & Story' concept have been simplified:\n\n*   **Visual Storytelling:** The reliance on detailed visual storytelling and character animations is completely removed. Story elements are presented through concise, text-based dialogue boxes and level transition messages, as per conversion notes.\n*   **Renovation and Decorative Options:** The intricate system of choosing specific flooring, furniture, and decorative options is abstracted. Instead, 'renovation tasks' are tied directly to level progression, and the completion is described in text rather than being visually selectable or rendered in detail. The game does not visually represent specific decorative choices.\n*   **Hundreds of Unique Merge Items:** The number of unique merge item types and their merge levels is reduced to a more manageable set (e.g., 6-8 base item types, each with 5-6 merge levels) to simplify asset creation and logic.\n*   **Mystery Elements:** The complex uncovering of island secrets and character interactions is streamlined. Clues are delivered as simple text snippets within level descriptions or transition screens, rather than through branching dialogue or elaborate puzzle mechanics.\n*   **Complex UI/UX & Inventory:** Intricate UI elements, inventory management systems, and character relationship tracking are omitted. The UI focuses solely on the merge board, active orders, and essential game information.\n*   **Multiplayer Features:** Not applicable, the original game is single-player and this implementation remains single-player.\n*   **Currency System:** While currency is mentioned, its function is simplified. It serves primarily as an abstract 'progression point' counter that, when reaching certain thresholds, automatically triggers the next story segment/level. Players do not actively spend currency on items or renovations within this implementation.",
  "implementation_notes": "The game will be implemented primarily using p5.js for all rendering, user input handling, and game state management. Matter.js is not required for this grid-based merge puzzle as it doesn't necessitate complex physics interactions.\n\n**Core Implementation Focus:**\n*   **Grid System:** A robust 2D array structure (`let grid = []`) to manage item placement and states. Each grid cell needs to store an object or a reference to an item object (`{id, level, x, y, sprite}`).\n*   **Mouse Input:** Careful handling of `mouseIsPressed`, `pmouseX`, `pmouseY`, `mouseX`, `mouseY` to accurately track item selection, dragging, and dropping.\n*   **Item Drawing:** Each item needs its own drawing function that uses its level to determine its visual representation (shape, color, or sprite).\n*   **Order Management:** An array of objects to track current orders, and functions to add, remove, and check completion status of orders.\n*   **Game State Management:** A `gameState` variable (e.g., `MENU`, `PLAYING`) and a `switch` statement in `draw()` to handle logic and rendering specific to each state.\n*   **Scoring & High Score:** Implement `localStorage` to save and load the player's high score. The current score and level indicator must be persistently displayed on-screen during `PLAYING_STATE` at the specified positions.\n*   **Level Progression:** Levels advance by fulfilling a specific quantity of orders per level. The game should auto-transition after a visual 'Level Complete' screen.\n*   **Randomness:** Use `random()` functions for item generation and initial board setup.\n*   **Animations:** Simple animation functions (e.g., `lerp` for smooth movement, `sin` for pulsing effects, `alpha` adjustment for fading) will enhance visual feedback."
}
</task>

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8" />
    <style>
      html, body { margin: 0; padding: 0; height: 100%; overflow: hidden; background: #222; }
      body { display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center; align-items: center; }
      canvas { border: 1px solid #333; width: 600px !important; height: 400px !important; }
      .control-buttons { display: flex; gap: 10px; margin-bottom: 10px; flex-wrap: wrap; justify-content: center; }
      .control-button { padding: 8px 16px; cursor: pointer; background: #444; color: #fff; border: none; border-radius: 4px; }
      .control-button.active { background: #007bff; }
    </style>
  </head>
  <body>
    <div style="text-align: center; margin-bottom: 20px;">
      <h1 id="gameTitle" style="color: #fff; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; margin-bottom: 10px;">{game_title}</h1>

Harbor Merge Mystery

Welcome to Harbor Merge Mystery! You've inherited a dilapidated harbor restaurant and must restore it to its former glory while uncovering the island's secrets. Merge identical items on the grid to create higher-level dishes, then fulfill customer orders by dragging completed items to the Order Zone. Progress through 5 challenging levels, each bringing you closer to the truth. Watch out - if your board fills up with no possible merges, it's game over!

ENTER: Start the game from the main menu | ARROW KEYS: Navigate through grid cells (for keyboard control mode) | SPACE: Select/drop item at cursor position (keyboard mode) or toggle pause | ESC: Pause/unpause the game | R: Restart and return to main menu | Z: Confirm item merge or order fulfillment (keyboard mode)
